ja-gram-n3-1565-fill-2: He was mid-action — leaning in for a kiss — when his mom walked in and stopped it. The attempt was real but never completed.
ja-gram-n3-1671-fill-0: The push happened at the exact moment of stepping off — the action was cut off right before completion by an outside event.
ja-gram-n3-1671-fill-1: Dad's call arrived at the precise instant of heading out the door, interrupting the action just before it could happen.
ja-gram-n3-1671-fill-3: The confession was already in motion when a friend's sudden arrival broke the moment and stopped it from happening.
ja-gram-n3-609-fill-0: Two actions linked in sequence — first bringing the bowl up, then shoveling rice in. The て-form bridges these steps smoothly.
ja-gram-n3-609-fill-1: Two qualities of the car are listed together: red AND large. The て-form strings adjectives into one description.
ja-gram-n3-609-fill-2: Going to the bookshop and buying it are two steps in one trip. The て-form connects them as a natural sequence.
ja-gram-n3-609-fill-3: Eating melon and drinking coffee are two things done one after the other. The て-form shows this simple sequence.
ja-gram-n3-609-fill-4: Walking is the method of coming — the て-form connects the means (walking) to the action (coming) as one continuous flow.
ja-gram-n3-1707-fill-0: The speaker wants to treat someone but isn't locked into a specific drink — a coke or whatever else sounds good.
ja-gram-n3-1707-fill-2: The speaker saw something animal-like but isn't certain what — monkeys, or something of that sort.
ja-gram-n3-1707-fill-3: The request is open-ended — a cold drink, whatever kind is available, would be welcome.
ja-gram-n3-1707-fill-4: The desire is for some kind of sport or activity, with baseball as the vague example rather than a firm demand.
ja-gram-n3-1781-fill-0: Two iPad models are presented as options — Mini or Air — but neither matters because both outcomes lead to the same result: wanting it.
ja-gram-n3-1781-fill-1: Comfortable or not, the speaker doesn't care either way. The only thing that actually matters is cheap rent.
ja-gram-n3-733-fill-0: This is a formal way to say 'who is a doctor' before a name, like a title. である gives the sentence a written or elevated tone.
ja-gram-n3-733-fill-1: The sentence sets up a hypothetical — 'even if that is so' — and である provides the formal version of 'to be' inside the conditional.
ja-gram-n3-733-fill-2: The sentence is a formal statement about the challenge of always being honest. である carries the written, philosophical tone this statement needs.
ja-gram-n3-638-fill-0: 変 is a na-adjective, so it needs な to connect it to the noun 人. This is the standard way na-adjectives modify nouns.
ja-gram-n3-638-fill-1: きれい is a na-adjective describing the legs. The な connects it directly to the noun that follows.
ja-gram-n3-638-fill-2: 静か is a na-adjective. It needs な to link it to the noun レストラン — this is how na-adjectives modify nouns.
ja-gram-n3-1657-fill-0: The smile looks forced and fake. わざとらしい sits before the noun 笑い to describe it as something performed, not genuine.
ja-gram-n3-1657-fill-1: His speaking style comes across as put-on and unnatural — わざとらしい captures that affected, over-the-top quality.
ja-gram-n3-1657-fill-2: The injury seems staged rather than real. わざとらしい expresses that suspicious, too-convenient quality of his behavior.
ja-gram-n3-640-fill-0: 聖なる is a poetic/archaic way to say 'sacred' — なる is used here instead of な for a dramatic, literary feel.
ja-gram-n3-640-fill-1: The subtitle 'A New Hope' uses 新たなる — an older, more ceremonial way to say 'new,' fitting the epic tone of the film.
ja-gram-n3-1587-fill-0: The speaker uses もとい to catch and correct themselves mid-sentence — they said 'date' but immediately stepped back to the less romantic word 'offline meeting.'
ja-gram-n3-974-fill-0: He credited the source of his success — his education. によると frames that attribution, pointing to the basis for his conclusion.
ja-gram-n3-974-fill-1: The information about the movie comes from a friend's account. によると signals that this is secondhand, sourced from that conversation.
ja-gram-n3-974-fill-2: Multiple news agencies are the named sources here. によると introduces the information as coming from those specific outlets.
ja-gram-n3-974-fill-3: Credit for the invention is attributed to Edison — によると marks this as received knowledge, not the speaker's direct claim.
ja-gram-n3-974-fill-4: The cost estimate comes from his calculation. によると sets up his figures as the source of the stated amount.
ja-gram-n3-1145-fill-0: Mentioning 'Switzerland' prompts a mental image. と言うと sets up that 'when you hear this topic' trigger for asking what comes to mind.
ja-gram-n3-1145-fill-1: The speaker explains why he wasn't fired by introducing the reason. と言うと frames this as 'to state the reason' — a clarifying pivot.
ja-gram-n3-1145-fill-2: Whatever comes up, that person inserts himself. と言うと works here as 'whenever anything is brought up,' covering any topic.
ja-gram-n3-1669-fill-0: 旨 quotes the substance of what the prosecutor said — not the exact words, but the key point that the suspect believed and acted on.
ja-gram-n3-1710-fill-0: The speaker is recalling a past impression and asking whether they remember it correctly — a soft, uncertain check on memory.
ja-gram-n3-1710-fill-1: The speaker can't quite place when it was. たっけ turns the question into a gentle memory search rather than a direct query.
ja-gram-n3-1710-fill-2: The speaker lost track of where they stopped. たっけ signals they're trying to retrieve that point from memory.
ja-gram-n3-1710-fill-3: The speaker is asking someone else to remind them — they've forgotten John's address and たっけ softens the question with a 'wasn't it…?' tone.
ja-gram-n3-1710-fill-4: The speaker genuinely can't remember what they ate. たっけ is used for self-directed memory searches like this.
ja-gram-n3-1787-fill-0: The speaker is amazed — eating a whole cake that big, alone, seems almost impossible. よくも～ものだ expresses that disbelief and mild outrage.
ja-gram-n3-779-fill-0: He missed the train and now regrets not coming earlier. べき expresses the rational thing he should have done but didn't.
ja-gram-n3-779-fill-1: Reading widely is presented as the proper, sensible thing to do. べき carries a moral or educational 'ought to' that fits this advice.
ja-gram-n3-779-fill-2: Relying on others is framed as the wrong approach. べきでない is the natural way to say 'you shouldn't' when it's about rational judgment.
ja-gram-n3-779-fill-3: How to live is the question — べき here frames it as 'the way one ought to live,' a philosophical should rather than a rule.
ja-gram-n3-779-fill-4: The speaker wants to know the right time to depart. べき asks for what would be the proper or best choice.
ja-gram-n3-757-fill-0: The speaker really wants to see that movie — たい attached to the verb stem expresses this personal desire directly.
ja-gram-n3-757-fill-1: The desire to buy the car is genuine, but money is the obstacle. たい shows the want clearly even when it can't be fulfilled.
ja-gram-n3-757-fill-2: This is asking what the other person would like to do — たい on the verb stem asks about their personal wish or preference.
ja-gram-n3-1772-fill-0: Eating vegetables is framed as a firm requirement, not just advice. なければなりません is the polite form for a strong obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1772-fill-1: Buying eggs is something that has to happen — it's a necessary errand. なければなりません states that obligation in polite speech.
ja-gram-n3-1772-fill-2: Daily Japanese study is framed as something the speaker must do. なければなりません expresses this as a firm, polite obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1154-fill-3: The speaker is asserting that something cannot possibly be true. そんな筈はない is a set phrase rejecting an expected outcome as impossible.
ja-gram-n3-1661-fill-0: Harassing animals is prohibited. てはいけません is the standard polite form for expressing something is not allowed.
ja-gram-n3-1661-fill-1: Smoking here is not permitted — the no-smoking rule applies. てはいけません states this prohibition politely.
ja-gram-n3-1661-fill-3: Parking bicycles here is forbidden. てはいけない states this rule as a clear prohibition.
ja-gram-n3-1661-fill-4: The person was told not to eat salty food — a prohibition issued for health reasons. てはいけない delivers that rule clearly.
ja-gram-n3-1709-fill-0: Homework is something that must get done. なければならない states this as a plain, neutral obligation in everyday speech.
ja-gram-n3-1709-fill-1: Studying Japanese is framed as something the speaker has to do — a personal obligation stated plainly.
ja-gram-n3-1709-fill-2: Final exams are unavoidable this week. なければならない expresses this as a necessary, non-optional obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1709-fill-3: Breakfast is framed as something the speaker has to eat — a regular, necessary obligation stated simply.
ja-gram-n3-1709-fill-4: The speaker needs to leave around 10. なければならない states this as a time-bound obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1519-fill-0: Those involved bear a responsibility to come clean. せねばならない gives this a formal, weighty tone fitting a serious obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1519-fill-1: Finding new markets is a business necessity. せねばならない frames this as a pressing formal duty the team must fulfill.
ja-gram-n3-1519-fill-2: The goal must be reached no matter what. せねばならない adds a determined, serious tone to this obligation.
ja-gram-n3-1519-fill-3: Respecting elders is framed as a moral duty. せねばならない gives this principle a formal, principled weight.
ja-gram-n3-1519-fill-4: Keeping up with him requires hard work. せねばならない expresses the effort as a firm and necessary commitment.
ja-gram-n3-1302-fill-1: Somebody made someone else do something, which counts as stubborn or pushy behavior. させる marks that causal force.
ja-gram-n3-1302-fill-2: The son needs to be made independent — the parent should cause that to happen. させる frames the parent as the one driving the change.
ja-gram-n3-1302-fill-3: The speaker doesn't want to cause you to worry — させる shows that the worry would be caused by the speaker's actions.
ja-gram-n3-1302-fill-4: Despite all efforts, the speaker couldn't get him to believe them — させる shows the failed attempt to cause belief in someone else.
ja-gram-n3-1734-fill-0: The speaker is checking their understanding of what was said — ということですね confirms 'so your point is that this happened gradually?'
ja-gram-n3-1720-fill-0: Asking someone to give you a drink in a polite, slightly formal way. くださいませんか adds a soft, requesting tone.
ja-gram-n3-1720-fill-1: Politely asking someone to introduce themselves in English. くださいませんか makes the request courteous rather than direct.
ja-gram-n3-1720-fill-2: Asking to borrow a book in a considerate, polite way. くださいませんか softens the request into a gentle question.
ja-gram-n3-1720-fill-3: Politely asking for another serving. くださいませんか frames it as a courteous, tentative request.
ja-gram-n3-1720-fill-4: Asking someone to explain more clearly, phrased as a polite request rather than a demand.
ja-gram-n3-1717-fill-0: The speaker is inviting someone to sit down warmly. どうぞ adds a welcoming, 'please go right ahead' feeling to the invitation.
ja-gram-n3-1717-fill-1: The speaker is offering snacks freely and encouraging the listener to help themselves. どうぞ signals open permission.
ja-gram-n3-1717-fill-2: The speaker is asking people to stay seated — どうぞ here softens the instruction into a warm, polite gesture.
ja-gram-n3-1717-fill-3: The speaker is inviting someone to stay and relax without hurry. どうぞ adds a generous, welcoming tone.
ja-gram-n3-1717-fill-4: The speaker is giving someone permission to go ahead and speak. どうぞ signals 'you have the floor — please proceed.'
ja-gram-n3-1598-fill-0: The speaker wants someone else to relay a message on their behalf. と伝えていただけませんか is the set phrase for this indirect, polite request.
ja-gram-n3-1130-fill-1: Calling first had a concrete purpose: confirming his arrival. ために links the phone call directly to that specific goal.
ja-gram-n3-1130-fill-2: Old age is the direct cause of death here. ために in this context marks the reason or cause, not a purpose.
ja-gram-n3-1130-fill-3: The question asks what function the key serves. 何のために asks 'for what purpose' — pointing to its intended use.
ja-gram-n3-1130-fill-4: There's no dictionary designed to serve foreigners' needs. ための marks the intended beneficiary or purpose the dictionary would fulfill.
ja-gram-n3-1504-fill-0: He lived in Japan but never made the change to speaking Japanese. ようになる marks this as a shift in ability that was expected but didn't happen.
ja-gram-n3-1504-fill-1: The child reached a new stage — now she does it herself. ようになる marks this as a gradual change into a new habit.
ja-gram-n3-1504-fill-2: The baby hasn't started smiling yet, but soon will reach that point. ようになる describes the coming shift into a new behavior.
ja-gram-n3-1504-fill-3: Coming to Japan brought a change — eating tofu became normal now. ようになる captures that transition from 'didn't do it' to 'now does it.'
ja-gram-n3-1504-fill-4: Mr. Renz reached the point of being able to speak Japanese. ようになる marks that he got there through gradual change.
ja-gram-n3-1482-fill-0: The thing being talked about is nothing more than a plan — not a done deal. 予定 is precisely the word for that scheduled, planned-but-not-guaranteed state.
ja-gram-n3-1482-fill-1: Finishing the assignment tomorrow is scheduled and intended. 予定 marks this as a concrete plan, not just a vague wish.
ja-gram-n3-1482-fill-2: Going home next week is the plan. 予定 signals this is scheduled and intended, not yet decided in the moment.
ja-gram-n3-1482-fill-3: Marrying her next year is a definite plan the speaker has made. 予定 marks it as something scheduled and committed to.
ja-gram-n3-1629-fill-0: The weight gain prompted a decision to change eating habits. ことにする is used when you make a choice for yourself about what you will do.
ja-gram-n3-1629-fill-1: 合格する is the target state the speaker wants to reach. ように sets up the goal in order to justify studying harder.
ja-gram-n3-1632-fill-0: Becoming a doctor was the plan, but it didn't happen. 積もりだった looks back at a past intention that was never carried out.
ja-gram-n3-1632-fill-1: The joke was meant as a joke, not taken that way. 積もりだった describes the original intention behind an action.
ja-gram-n3-1632-fill-2: The Hawaii trip was planned but didn't happen. 積もりだった reflects on what the speaker had intended before things fell through.
ja-gram-n3-1632-fill-3: He had intended to marry her — the plan existed — but he didn't follow through. つもりだった captures that gap between intention and outcome.
ja-gram-n3-1632-fill-4: Nobody was supposed to visit and the speaker wasn't planning to go out either. つもりだった reflects what was intended before the day unfolded.
ja-gram-n3-1268-fill-0: Using it like the example is the suggested approach. ように expresses 'in the manner of' — follow the example as your target.
ja-gram-n3-1268-fill-1: Taking care is the desired behavior to prevent forgetting. ように connects the caution to the goal of not leaving things behind.
ja-gram-n3-1268-fill-2: The request is for people to delete the data. ように here frames a polite indirect instruction — 'please do it so that this happens.'
ja-gram-n3-1268-fill-3: The speaker is wishing for someone's recovery. ように here expresses a hope or prayer directed at a desired future state.
ja-gram-n3-1268-fill-4: Making things easier to manage is the goal. ように sets up the target state that the coming changes are designed to achieve.
ja-gram-n3-1769-fill-0: Quitting smoking is the decision that was made. ことにする marks this as a personal, conscious choice the speaker has committed to.
ja-gram-n3-737-fill-0: One person is pushing back and the other asks a clarifying question. の here is a casual, slightly pointed question marker used in conversation.
ja-gram-n3-737-fill-1: The speaker is surprised the shop is already closed. の turns the statement into a casual, slightly incredulous question.
ja-gram-n3-737-fill-2: A casual question asking why someone doesn't have a girlfriend. の makes it sound conversational and informal, not harsh.
ja-gram-n3-737-fill-3: The speaker is declining with a casual explanation. の here softens the statement into an informal, conversational explanation-as-question.
ja-gram-n3-737-fill-4: One speaker asks what's wrong in a soft, concerned way. の makes the question feel personal and gentle rather than blunt.
ja-gram-n3-893-fill-0: It doesn't matter either way — known or not. かどうか packages both possibilities (yes or no) into one unit for the point to apply to.
ja-gram-n3-893-fill-1: The speaker wants to know if Lucy can speak Japanese or not. かどうか asks about both the yes and no outcomes inside an indirect question.
ja-gram-n3-893-fill-2: The question is whether he has a girlfriend or not. かどうか bundles both possibilities into a single indirect question.
ja-gram-n3-893-fill-3: He was asking about your health — whether you were well or not. かどうか embeds that yes/no question into the reported inquiry.
ja-gram-n3-893-fill-4: The test is whether the shoes fit or not. かどうか frames both outcomes as what needs to be checked.
ja-gram-n3-693-fill-0: Whether or not they wrote it, the author credit stays the same. か embeds the question of authorship as a subordinate clause.
ja-gram-n3-693-fill-1: The speaker asks if the listener knows who they are — か embeds the identity question inside the larger sentence.
ja-gram-n3-693-fill-2: The timing of the trip is still unknown. か turns 'when' into an embedded question that the speaker can't yet answer.
ja-gram-n3-693-fill-3: Asking who went out with them — か turns the who-question into a clause that the listener is asked to explain.
ja-gram-n3-693-fill-4: The speaker asked where the documents are — か embeds that where-question as reported speech inside the verb 聞いた.
ja-gram-n3-749-fill-0: The speaker is looking at the shoes and wondering about the price — not asking someone else, just thinking aloud with uncertainty.
ja-gram-n3-749-fill-1: The speaker is worrying about a hypothetical — what would happen if they miss the deadline. かな voices that open-ended personal concern.
ja-gram-n3-749-fill-2: The speaker wonders about the seriousness of the illness, expressing uncertainty and mild worry to themselves.
ja-gram-n3-749-fill-3: The elbow hurts and the speaker muses aloud about whether to go to the hospital — a self-directed wondering about the best next step.
ja-gram-n3-749-fill-4: The speaker is unsure whether early July is too early for the beach — かな voices that personal, uncertain wondering.
ja-gram-n3-714-fill-0: A very casual 'who is it?' — だい makes the question sound familiar and slightly gruff, typical of informal male speech.
ja-gram-n3-714-fill-1: A parent or older person asking what someone is sneaking around doing — だい adds that informal, slightly scolding tone.
ja-gram-n3-714-fill-2: Someone is staying up late and being told to go to bed. だい makes the question feel like an annoyed, casual demand for an explanation.
ja-gram-n3-714-fill-3: A curious, slightly searching question about what someone really wants to know. だい gives it that casual, probing informal feel.
ja-gram-n3-1774-fill-0: The speaker is wondering who left the TV on — a soft, self-directed mystery. かしら gives it a gentle, feminine wondering tone.
ja-gram-n3-1774-fill-1: The speaker is wondering about the price to themselves. かしら gives this a soft, feminine 'I wonder' quality rather than a direct question.
ja-gram-n3-1773-fill-0: The speaker asks a simple yes/no question about whether it's wrapping up soon. かい makes it sound casual and friendly, typical of relaxed male speech.
